text: Chapter 3 introduces five foundational right-hand techniques for guitar: the flat pick, thumb pick with fingers, flat pick with fingers, fingers and thumb with no pick, and thumb only. The chapter establishes that players need not restrict themselves to a single technique, as many accomplished guitarists employ different approaches for different sounds. Chet Atkins exemplifies the thumb-pick-with-fingers approach. The load-bearing constraint is that certain chords demand finger-based techniques for playability; specifically, 4-note chords in the flat-pick-with-fingers style require the pick on the bass note and one finger per remaining string with the little finger taking the highest note, 5- and 6-note chords require brushing (one or two fingers covering multiple strings), and 3-note chords use only 2 fingers plus pick or 3 fingers alone. This taxonomy of techniques and their chord-specific rules forms the foundation for all subsequent right-hand instruction.
